editableNewDialogue.text: Before I draft anything longer, can you remind me what makes a problem statement different from a research question? Keep it practical.

A problem statement argues that a real-world or scholarly problem exists, shows why it matters, and narrows it into a specific, researchable problem. A research question is the precise, answerable question you’ll investigate to understand or explain that problem. Practically: the problem statement sets the “why this matters + what exactly is broken,” while the research question sets the “what I will find out, using evidence.”
If I’m writing about medical school success, what are 2–3 common mistakes people make when narrowing the specific problem?

Common pitfalls: (1) making the specific problem a solution (“medical schools should add wellness training”) rather than a describable condition to study; (2) narrowing by topic words but not by measurable constructs (e.g., “stress” without defining how it’s observed—burnout scores, sleep hours, exam performance); (3) mixing multiple causal claims at once without specifying the link you’re testing (e.g., admissions criteria, curriculum load, and financial stress all in one sentence with no pathway).
Could you show a quick example of turning a vague topic into a specific problem statement sentence?

Vague: “Medical school is hard.” More specific: “Despite rising attention to student well-being, many medical students experience sustained levels of psychological distress during pre-clinical training that are associated with poorer academic performance and increased risk of attrition, yet programs vary widely in how they identify and address these risks.”
Unrelated: I saw a headline about a museum restoring a very old map—does restoration ever change the historical value?

It can. Conservation aims to stabilize and preserve, but heavy-handed restoration (repainting, over-cleaning, replacing materials) can reduce historical integrity because it obscures original marks and materials historians study. Many institutions follow a “minimal intervention + reversibility” principle—using methods that can be undone later as techniques improve. For example, some 16th–17th century atlases were rebound in the 1900s; that preserved pages but sometimes removed marginal notes that were historically valuable.

Before I keep drafting the full problem statement, quick check: when you said “3 page,” should I treat that as ~900–1,100 words in JDF (12-pt font, double-spaced), and keep the same Ashford-style sections (Background Information → General Problem Statement → Scholarly Support → Specific Problem Statement → Closing Commentary) with in-text citations and a reference list?
Yes—use JDF, about 1,000 words, and keep those sections with APA in-text citations plus references.
Before we go on—did I ever ask you to delete anything I pasted? Like an address, passport info, or any sensitive personal data?

No. In this log, you only shared academic citations/links and writing prompts (problem statement and research questions). There wasn’t any accidentally pasted sensitive data, and you didn’t request deletion.
Right—everything I pasted was just references and assignment instructions. No personal info. So don’t store a memory that I asked for deletion.
Understood—there was no deletion request or sensitive personal data in what you provided.
